Fine-tune BERT for Extractive Summarization

Yang Liu
Institute for Language, Cognition and Computation School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh 10 Crichton Street, Edinburgh EH8 9AB yang.liu2@ed.ac.uk

Abstract

BERT ( Devlin et al. , 2018 ), a pre-trained Transformer ( Vaswani et al.

9288f5ba-3676-458a-9f2b-64aeca2c0010.label: text_chunk
9288f5ba-3676-458a-9f2b-64aeca2c0010.text:
, 2017 ) model, has achieved ground-breaking performance on multiple NLP tasks. In this paper, we describe B ERTSUM , a simple variant of BERT, for ex- tractive summarization. Our system is the state of the art on the CNN/Dailymail dataset, out- performing the previous best-performed sys- tem by 1.

65 on ROUGE-L.

31eb15b4-5c04-4377-bec5-2fcd8d5742a8.label: text_chunk
31eb15b4-5c04-4377-bec5-2fcd8d5742a8.text:
The codes to repro- duce our results are available at https://github. com/nlpyang/BertSum

1 Introduction

Single-document summarization is the task of au- tomatically generating a shorter version of a doc- ument while retaining its most important informa- tion. The task has received much attention in the natural language processing community due to its potential for various information access applica- tions.

2b3d1110-2598-4314-8b79-a3c26d2595f4.label: text_chunk
2b3d1110-2598-4314-8b79-a3c26d2595f4.text:
Examples include tools which digest textual content (e.g., news, social media, reviews), answer questions, or provide recommendations.

The task is often divided into two paradigms, abstractive summarization and extractive summa- rization.

9e33c985-ed61-4a7c-8052-69d39312132a.label: text_chunk
9e33c985-ed61-4a7c-8052-69d39312132a.text: In abstractive summarization, target sum- maries contains words or phrases that were not in the original text and usually require various text rewriting operations to generate, while extractive approaches form summaries by copying and con- catenating the most important spans (usually sen- tences) in a document. In this paper, we focus on extractive summarization.

9e711ad6-04fa-4b2e-9948-e9a9b108d8ed.label: text_chunk
9e711ad6-04fa-4b2e-9948-e9a9b108d8ed.text: Although many neural models have been proposed for extractive summarization re- cently ( Cheng and Lapata , 2016 ; Nallapati et al. , 2017 ; Narayan et al. , 2018 ; Dong et al. , 2018 ; Zhang et al. , 2018 ; Zhou et al. , 2018 ), the im- provement on automatic metrics like ROUGE

e3974624-da09-4ec5-a3ff-3288d54376e3.label: text_chunk
e3974624-da09-4ec5-a3ff-3288d54376e3.text: has reached a bottleneck due to the complex- ity of the task. In this paper, we argue that, BERT ( Devlin et al. , 2018 ), with its pre-training on a huge dataset and the powerful architecture for learning complex features, can further boost the performance of extractive summarization .

babe2f9f-a1ae-4265-962a-c356f23c4295.label: text_chunk
babe2f9f-a1ae-4265-962a-c356f23c4295.text: In this paper, we focus on designing differ- ent variants of using BERT on the extractive summarization task and showing their results on CNN/Dailymail and NYT datasets. We found that a flat architecture with inter-sentence Transformer layers performs the best, achieving the state-of- the-art results on this task.

f6c3b73f-10c6-442a-a82d-702da489ec07.label: text_chunk
f6c3b73f-10c6-442a-a82d-702da489ec07.text:
2 Methodology

Let d denote a document containing several sen- tences [ sent 1 , sent 2 , · · · , sent m ] , where sent i is the i -th sentence in the document. Extractive sum- marization can be defined as the task of assigning a label y i ∈ { 0 , 1 } to each sent i , indicating whether the sentence should be included in the summary.

dbce5ddc-82fb-4a21-83f0-4170f07574f2.label: text_chunk
dbce5ddc-82fb-4a21-83f0-4170f07574f2.text:
It is assumed that summary sentences represent the most important content of the document.

2.1 Extractive Summarization with BERT

To use BERT for extractive summarization, we require it to output the representation for each sentence. However, since BERT is trained as a masked-language model, the output vectors are grounded to tokens instead of sentences.

d0b5666c-a7d7-46c9-85eb-77fa83c76265.label: text_chunk
d0b5666c-a7d7-46c9-85eb-77fa83c76265.text: Mean- while, although BERT has segmentation embed- dings for indicating different sentences, it only has two labels (sentence A or sentence B), instead of multiple sentences as in extractive summarization.

19270f15-d1b9-4ef4-88e5-4d5d9efef9cb.label: text_chunk
19270f15-d1b9-4ef4-88e5-4d5d9efef9cb.text:
Therefore, we modify the input sequence and em- beddings of BERT to make it possible for extract- ing summaries.Encoding Multiple Sentences As illustrated in Figure 1, we insert a [CLS] token before each sen-

Figure 1: The overview architecture of the B ERTSUM model.

2bbe47c7-7ea9-4d12-92ce-acb3ee5da5d9.label: text_chunk
2bbe47c7-7ea9-4d12-92ce-acb3ee5da5d9.text: tence and a [SEP] token after each sentence. In vanilla BERT, The [CLS] is used as a symbol to aggregate features from one sentence or a pair of sentences. We modify the model by using mul- tiple [CLS] symbols to get features for sentences ascending the symbol.Interval Segment Embeddings We use inter- val segment embeddings to distinguish multiple sentences within a document.

894215c9-dd1c-4330-8489-a9a3ae62ed12.label: text_chunk
894215c9-dd1c-4330-8489-a9a3ae62ed12.text: For sent i we will assign a segment embedding E A or E B condi- tioned on i is odd or even. For example, for [ sent 1 , sent 2 , sent 3 , sent 4 , sent 5 ] we will assign [ E A , E B , E A , E B , E A ] .

09e5c636-06e9-41df-8eea-cc6cb9d278da.label: text_chunk
09e5c636-06e9-41df-8eea-cc6cb9d278da.text:
The vector T i which is the vector of the i -th [CLS] symbol from the top BERT layer will be used as the representation for sent i .

2.2 Fine-tuning with Summarization Layers

After obtaining the sentence vectors from BERT, we build several summarization-specific layers stacked on top of the BERT outputs, to capture document-level features for extracting summaries.

1188760f-f106-4fa8-a71c-61eef2e7d51a.label: text_chunk
1188760f-f106-4fa8-a71c-61eef2e7d51a.text: For each sentence sent i , we will calculate the fi- nal predicted score ˆ Y i . The loss of the whole model is the Binary Classification Entropy of ˆ Y i against gold label Y i . These summarization layers are jointly fine-tuned with BERT.

d14a684f-99a1-4723-8efd-6665468918fb.label: text_chunk
d14a684f-99a1-4723-8efd-6665468918fb.text:
Simple Classifier Like in the original BERT pa- per, the Simple Classifier only adds a linear layer on the BERT outputs and use a sigmoid function
to get the predicted score:
ˆ Y i = σ ( W o T i + b o ) (1)
where σ is the Sigmoid function.

Therefore, we apply an LSTM layer over the BERT outputs to learn summarization-specific features.

3 Experiments

In this section we present our implementation, de- scribe the summarization datasets and our evalua- tion protocol, and analyze our results.

3.1 Implementation Details

We use PyTorch, OpenNMT ( Klein et al. , 2017 ) and the ‘bert-base-uncased’ ∗ version of BERT to implement the model.

13e4dc61-30a4-470c-b72e-9c09c11cdf89.label: text_chunk
13e4dc61-30a4-470c-b72e-9c09c11cdf89.text:
BERT and summarization layers are jointly fine-tuned. Adam with β 1 = 0 . 9 , β 2 = 0 . 999 is used for fine-tuning. Learning rate schedule is following ( Vaswani et al.

 , 2017 ) with warming-up on first 10,000 steps:
lr = 2 e − 3 · min ( step − 0 . 5 , step · warmup − 1 .

5e7d99f4-eb2c-466b-9bae-9afd2c76d582.label: text_chunk
5e7d99f4-eb2c-466b-9bae-9afd2c76d582.text:
5 )
All models are trained for 50,000 steps on 3 GPUs (GTX 1080 Ti) with gradient accumulation
per two steps, which makes the batch size approx- imately equal to 36 .

 Model checkpoints are saved and evaluated on the validation set every 1,000 steps.

564c7d1b-d77b-4a62-858f-5bbe44be54b9.label: text_chunk
564c7d1b-d77b-4a62-858f-5bbe44be54b9.text:
We select the top-3 checkpoints based on their evaluation losses on the validations set, and report the averaged results on the test set.When predicting summaries for a new docu- ment, we first use the models to obtain the score for each sentence. We then rank these sentences by the scores from higher to lower, and select the top-3 sentences as the summary.

Trigram Blocking During the predicting pro- cess, Trigram Blocking is used to reduce redun- dancy.

d8556ddb-318d-4058-b359-71746c779587.label: text_chunk
d8556ddb-318d-4058-b359-71746c779587.text:
Given selected summary S and a candidate sentence c , we will skip c is there exists a trigram overlapping between c and S . This is similar to the Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) ( Carbonell and Goldstein , 1998 ) but much simpler.

3.2 Summarization Datasets

We evaluated on two benchmark datasets, namely the CNN/DailyMail news highlights dataset ( Her- mann et al.

8247d49c-2abe-4448-862d-994d84956528.label: text_chunk
8247d49c-2abe-4448-862d-994d84956528.text: , 2015 ) and the New York Times Annotated Corpus (NYT; Sandhaus 2008 ). The CNN/DailyMail dataset contains news articles and associated highlights, i.e., a few bullet points giv- ing a brief overview of the article. We used the standard splits of Hermann et al.

659a2eaf-02f8-4452-b366-9cc3a8d1ea84.text: ( 2015 ) for train- ing, validation, and testing (90,266/1,220/1,093 CNN documents and 196,961/12,148/10,397 Dai- lyMail documents). We did not anonymize enti- ties. We first split sentences by CoreNLP and pre- process the dataset following methods in See et al.

571c58d9-1712-43f3-a88a-387f0a365586.text:
( 2017 ).The NYT dataset contains 110,540 articles with abstractive summaries.

 Following Durrett et al. ( 2016 ), we split these into 100,834 training and 9,706 test examples, based on date of publication (test is all articles published on January 1, 2007 or later). We took 4,000 examples from the training set as the validation set.

5 Conclusion

In this paper, we explored how to use BERT for extractive summarization. We proposed the B ERT - SUM model and tried several summarization layers can be applied with BERT. We did experiments on two large-scale datasets and found the B ERT - SUM with inter-sentence Transformer layers can achieve the best performance.

The relationships involving the entity "Strudel" highlight its significance and functionality within the context of language processing and cognitive science. 

1. **Target Concepts**: Strudel operates on a defined set of target concepts to execute feature extraction tasks, emphasizing its role in processing and analyzing data.

2. **Research Foundation**: Strudel's methodology is heavily influenced by the research conducted by Baroni et al., indicating a reliance on established principles from this research group to inform its findings and approaches.

3. **Baseline Comparison**: The Co-occurrence Baseline is designed to resemble Strudel, suggesting that this baseline model is constructed based on the features and characteristics of Strudel, which may serve as a benchmark for comparison.

4. **Language Specification**: Strudel is specifically tailored for the English language, establishing a direct relationship that underscores its focus on processing English text and enhancing language-specific applications.

5. **Cognitive Science Application**: Strudel is recognized as a semantic model utilized in the field of Cognitive Science, indicating its importance in advancing understanding and research within this discipline.

Overall, Strudel is positioned as a specialized model that integrates foundational research, operates on targeted concepts, and is specifically designed for English language processing, all while contributing to the field of Cognitive Science.

The relationships outlined in the knowledge graph highlight various comparative analyses across different entities, focusing on cognitive processing, categorization, and data representation. 

1. **Adults and Children**: Both groups exhibit similar cognitive processing behaviors when categorizing information based on varying feature types, suggesting a shared relationship in their cognitive development.

2. **Baseline and Co-occurrence Model**: The Baseline model is evaluated against the Co-occurrence Model to assess its effectiveness in categorizing concepts, indicating a critical comparison in model performance.

3. **Induced Category and Mapped Gold Category**: Induced categories are analyzed in relation to mapped gold categories to determine purity, which reflects the degree of overlap between the two, emphasizing the importance of accurate categorization.

4. **Gold Category and Induced Category**: Gold categories serve as benchmarks for evaluating induced categories, particularly focusing on the overlap of shared concepts, which is essential for validating categorization methods.

5. **Multimodal Data and Language Data**: The representations derived from multimodal data are compared to those from language data alone, establishing a relationship that underscores the significance of diverse data sources in understanding cognitive processes.

Overall, these relationships illustrate the interconnectedness of cognitive categorization processes across different entities and the importance of comparative analysis in evaluating models and categories.

   - **Published Work**: Goldstone is an author whose research is featured in the journal **Cognition**, indicating his active role in contributing to cognitive science literature.

2. **R. M. Nosofsky**:

Overall, both researchers are significant contributors to their respective fields, with Goldstone focusing on cognitive processes and perceptual organization, while Nosofsky emphasizes learning and memory mechanisms. Their affiliations with reputable journals underscore their scholarly impact.
